approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all subsets of the input array recursively, checking if any subset sums to the target. Use it for small input sizes due to exponential time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Subset Sum Existence via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution checking all subsets for target sum
ladders.explanation: The brute force recursion approach solves the subset sum existence problem by systematically exploring all possible subsets of the given array to find if any subset sums to the target value. This works because every element has two choices: either include it in the current subset or exclude it, leading to 2^n possible subsets. The intuition is that by recursively making these inclusion/exclusion decisions for each element, we can generate all subsets without explicitly storing them. The algorithm starts with the first element and recursively explores two branches: one where the element is included (subtracting its value from the target) and one where it's excluded (keeping the target unchanged). The base cases are: when target becomes 0 (success - subset found), when we've processed all elements (failure - no subset found), or when target becomes negative (failure - overshoot). The time complexity is O(2^n) because we explore 2^n subsets in the worst case. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion call stack depth. This approach is foundational for understanding exponential algorithms and serves as a stepping stone to more optimized solutions like dynamic programming. Common mistakes include not handling negative target values properly, forgetting base cases, and inefficiently passing arrays by value instead of using indices. The key insight is that recursion naturally captures the combinatorial nature of subset generation.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Subset Generation with Target Tracking
ladders.desc: Recursive function that generates subsets while tracking remaining target sum
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach - a recursive function that explores all subsets by making inclusion/exclusion decisions for each element while tracking how much of the target sum remains. The function takes three parameters: the array, current index position, and remaining target sum. The intuition is that we can think of the problem as a decision tree: at each element, we have two choices (include or exclude), and we need to explore both possibilities. When we include an element, we subtract its value from the target; when we exclude it, we keep the target unchanged. The function returns True if any path in this decision tree leads to target becoming 0. The algorithm: 1. Base case 1: if target == 0, return True (subset found). 2. Base case 2: if index >= array length, return False (processed all elements). 3. Base case 3: if target < 0, return False (overshot the target). 4. Recursive case: return (include_path OR exclude_path), where include_path = recursive_call(index+1, target-arr[index]) and exclude_path = recursive_call(index+1, target). Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(2^n) as it explores all subsets. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into the full problem by being the complete recursive solution - the full problem simply calls this function with initial parameters. Common mistakes include incorrect base case ordering, not handling negative targets properly, and passing arrays by value instead of reference. The key insight is that recursion naturally implements the combinatorial exploration of all subsets.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Decision Tree for Subset Exploration
ladders.desc: The decision tree model where each element choice branches into include/exclude paths
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is modeling the subset generation as a binary decision tree. Each node in this tree represents a decision point where we choose whether to include or exclude the current array element in our subset. The intuition is that every subset corresponds to exactly one path from root to leaf in this decision tree. For an array of n elements, the tree has depth n (one level per element) and 2^n leaves (one for each possible subset). At level i (corresponding to array element i), we have two branches: left branch includes element i (subtracting its value from target), right branch excludes element i (keeping target unchanged). We traverse this tree depth-first, checking at each leaf if the accumulated decisions result in target sum = 0. The algorithm: 1. Start at root with empty subset and full target. 2. For each element i from 0 to n-1: a. Take include branch: add element to subset, subtract from target, recurse to next element. b. Take exclude branch: keep subset and target unchanged, recurse to next element. 3. At leaf (after processing all elements), check if target = 0. Time complexity is O(2^n) for exploring all leaves. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the conceptual model that the recursive function implements. Common mistakes include not understanding the exponential nature, trying to store all subsets explicitly, or confusing depth-first with breadth-first traversal. The key insight is that the decision tree naturally captures all subset combinations without needing to generate them explicitly.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal with State
ladders.desc: Depth-first traversal of a binary tree while maintaining accumulated state
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a binary tree depth-first while carrying and updating state information (in our case, the remaining target sum). The intuition is that many recursive algorithms follow this pattern: we have a tree structure (explicit or implicit), we traverse it while maintaining some state, and we make decisions at each node that affect that state. In the subset sum context, the implicit tree is the decision tree, and the state is the remaining target sum. The algorithm: 1. Define a recursive function that takes current node (implied by index) and current state (target). 2. Base cases: handle terminal conditions (leaf nodes or invalid states). 3. Recursive case: for each child (include/exclude), update state appropriately and recurse. 4. Combine results from children (using OR in our case). Time complexity depends on tree size; for binary tree of depth n, it's O(2^n). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into the core logic by providing the traversal mechanism for the decision tree. Common mistakes include not properly updating/restoring state, infinite recursion from missing base cases, or incorrect combination of child results. The key insight is that recursion naturally implements depth-first tree traversal with state propagation.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Inclusion/Exclusion with Target Update
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of including or excluding an element from consideration
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is the atomic action in the subset sum algorithm: at each step, we decide whether to include the current element in our subset or exclude it, and correspondingly update the remaining target sum. The intuition is simple: if we include an element, its value contributes to our sum, so we subtract it from the target; if we exclude it, it doesn't contribute, so the target remains unchanged. Mathematically: include → new_target = old_target - element_value; exclude → new_target = old_target. This operation is performed at each recursive call. The algorithm: 1. Receive current element value and current target. 2. Make two copies of the problem state: one for inclusion, one for exclusion. 3. For inclusion branch: subtract element value from target. 4. For exclusion branch: keep target unchanged. 5. Pass both updated states to next recursive level. Time complexity for this operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the operation itself (though recursion creates multiple copies). This feeds into the building block as the state update rule in tree traversal. Common mistakes include modifying the original target instead of creating copies, incorrect subtraction, or confusing inclusion/exclusion logic. The key insight is that this simple operation, when repeated recursively, generates all possible subset combinations.

examples.trace:
Operation: Process element with value 5 when current target is 12.
Step 1: Create inclusion branch:
  Decision: Include element 5 in subset
  Update: new_target = current_target - element_value = 12 - 5 = 7
  Result: include_target = 7
Step 2: Create exclusion branch:
  Decision: Exclude element 5 from subset
  Update: new_target = current_target (unchanged) = 12
  Result: exclude_target = 12
Step 3: Both branches are now ready for further processing.
This demonstrates how a single element decision creates two divergent paths.

Operation: Process element with value 3 when current target is 3.
Step 1: Inclusion branch:
  Include element 3
  Update: new_target = 3 - 3 = 0
  Result: include_target = 0 (special case - target reached!)
Step 2: Exclusion branch:
  Exclude element 3
  Update: new_target = 3 (unchanged)
  Result: exclude_target = 3
Step 3: The inclusion branch has reached target 0, which means if we take this path and include element 3, we've found a subset that sums to the original target.
This shows how the operation can immediately identify a solution when element value equals current target.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Fundamental recursion concepts: function calling itself with termination conditions
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. The intuition is divide-and-conquer: break a big problem into smaller, similar problems until they become simple enough to solve directly. Every recursive function needs: 1. Base case(s): simple, non-recursive cases that stop the recursion (like when input is empty or target is reached). 2. Recursive case: where the function calls itself with modified parameters. 3. Progress toward base case: each recursive call must move closer to a base case to avoid infinite recursion. For subset sum, the base cases are: target becomes 0 (success), we've processed all elements (failure), or target becomes negative (failure). The recursive case splits into two calls: one including current element, one excluding it. Time complexity analysis involves solving recurrence relations; for binary recursion like this, it's typically exponential. Space complexity is the maximum depth of recursion calls. This feeds into all higher levels as the fundamental mechanism. Common mistakes include missing base cases (causing infinite recursion), not making progress toward base cases, or not combining results properly. The key insight is that recursion elegantly captures the self-similar nature of many problems.

examples.trace:
factorial(n): if n<=1 return 1 else return n * factorial(n-1)

Call factorial(4):
4 > 1, so return 4 * factorial(3)
  Call factorial(3):
  3 > 1, so return 3 * factorial(2)
    Call factorial(2):
    2 > 1, so return 2 * factorial(1)
      Call factorial(1):
      1 <= 1, so return 1 (BASE CASE)
    Now factorial(2) returns: 2 * 1 = 2
  Now factorial(3) returns: 3 * 2 = 6
Now factorial(4) returns: 4 * 6 = 24

Key observations:
1. Base case: n<=1 returns 1 (stops recursion)
2. Recursive case: n * factorial(n-1) (calls itself with smaller n)
3. Progress: each call reduces n by 1, moving toward base case
4. Results combine via multiplication as calls return

Problem: Count paths in 2-step staircase where you can take 1 or 2 steps at a time.
countSteps(n): if n<0 return 0, if n==0 return 1, else return countSteps(n-1) + countSteps(n-2)

Call countSteps(2):
n=2, not <0, not ==0, so recursive case:
return countSteps(1) + countSteps(0)

First branch: countSteps(1)
  n=1, not <0, not ==0, so recursive case:
  return countSteps(0) + countSteps(-1)
    countSteps(0): n==0, so return 1 (BASE CASE)
    countSteps(-1): n<0, so return 0 (BASE CASE)
  So countSteps(1) returns: 1 + 0 = 1

Second branch: countSteps(0)
  n==0, so return 1 (BASE CASE)

Now countSteps(2) returns: 1 + 1 = 2

This demonstrates:
1. Multiple base cases (n<0 and n==0)
2. Multiple recursive branches (n-1 and n-2)
3. Results combined with addition
4. Similar pattern to subset sum but with different base cases and combination

approaches.name: DP with Memoization O(n * sum)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store results of subproblems (index, current sum) to avoid recomputation. Suitable for medium-sized inputs where the target sum is not too large.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Subset Sum Existence with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to determine if any subset sums to target
ladders.explanation: The subset sum existence problem asks whether there exists a subset of a given array whose elements sum exactly to a target value. The DP with memoization approach solves this by breaking it into overlapping subproblems: at each index, we decide whether to include or exclude the current element in our subset. The key insight is that many subproblems (index, current_sum) are repeated, so we memoize results to avoid exponential recomputation. We define a recursive function subsetExists(index, current_sum) that returns True if we can achieve the target sum starting from index with current accumulated sum. The base cases are: if current_sum equals target, return True; if index exceeds array bounds or current_sum exceeds target, return False. Otherwise, we recursively explore two possibilities: include the current element (add it to current_sum) or exclude it (keep current_sum unchanged). We store results in a 2D memo dictionary keyed by (index, current_sum). The time complexity is O(n * sum) because we compute each unique (index, current_sum) pair once, where n is array length and sum is target sum. Space complexity is O(n * sum) for the memo storage. This approach is efficient for medium-sized inputs where target sum is not too large, avoiding the O(2^n) brute-force complexity. Connection to L1: This full solution implements the key recursive subroutine from L1 within the complete problem context. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (this solution assumes non-negative, but could be adapted), forgetting to memoize before returning, or incorrect base case ordering. Real code pattern: Use a dictionary for memo, recursive function with index and current_sum parameters, and check memo at start.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that checks subset sum existence from a given index and current sum
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the DP with memoization approach. It defines a function subsetExists(index, current_sum) that determines whether we can achieve the target sum starting from the given index in the array, given the current accumulated sum. The intuition is to treat the problem as a decision tree: at each step (index), we choose to either include the current element in our subset or exclude it. By recursively exploring both choices, we cover all possible subsets. Memoization is crucial because without it, the recursion would have exponential time complexity O(2^n) due to repeated calculations of the same subproblems. We use a dictionary memo where the key is a tuple (index, current_sum) and the value is the boolean result. At the start of the function, we check if (index, current_sum) is in memo; if yes, return the stored value. Otherwise, we compute the result by: first, checking base cases—if current_sum equals target, return True; if index is beyond array length or current_sum exceeds target, return False. Then, we recursively call subsetExists(index+1, current_sum + arr[index]) for the include case, and subsetExists(index+1, current_sum) for the exclude case. We store the logical OR of these two results in memo before returning. Time complexity for this sub-routine, when integrated with memoization across calls, is O(n * sum) because each unique (index, current_sum) pair is computed once. Space complexity is O(n * sum) for the memo storage. Connection to L0: This function is called from the main solution in L0 with initial parameters (0, 0). Connection to L2: The core logic of include/exclude decisions is implemented here. Common mistakes include incorrect base case order (e.g., checking index out of bounds before checking current_sum equals target), not memoizing before returning, or using a list for memo which can be inefficient for sparse states. Real code pattern: Define recursive function with memo dictionary as nonlocal or passed parameter.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Include/Exclude Decision with Recursion
ladders.desc: Understand the recursive decision to include or exclude each element in the subset
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the subset sum problem is the recursive decision-making process: for each element in the array, we have two choices—include it in the current subset or exclude it. This creates a binary decision tree where each path from root to leaf represents a unique subset. The intuition is that by exploring all possible combinations of includes and excludes, we can check every subset without explicitly enumerating all 2^n subsets (which would be inefficient). At each recursive call corresponding to an index i and current sum s, we consider: if we include arr[i], the new sum becomes s + arr[i], and we move to index i+1; if we exclude arr[i], the sum remains s, and we move to index i+1. We combine these possibilities using logical OR: a subset exists if either including or excluding the current element leads to a valid subset. This recursive decomposition reduces the problem to smaller subproblems: subsetExists(i, s) = subsetExists(i+1, s + arr[i]) OR subsetExists(i+1, s). The base cases stop the recursion: if s equals the target sum, return True (we found a subset); if i is beyond the array length, return False (no more elements to consider); if s exceeds the target sum, we can return False early (pruning) assuming non-negative numbers. This logic is efficient when combined with memoization, as it avoids recomputing overlapping subproblems. Time complexity without memoization is O(2^n), but with memoization it becomes O(n * sum). Space complexity for the recursion stack is O(n). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the recursive function of L1. Connection to L3: The building block of recursion is used here. Common mistakes include not pruning when s exceeds target (which can save time), incorrect base case handling for empty subsets (target sum 0 should return True), or misunderstanding the OR operation (we need existence, not count). Real code pattern: Use if-else conditions to handle base cases, then return recursive calls combined with OR.

OR exclude arr[0] → sum stays 0, go to index 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursion with State Parameters
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive function that takes state parameters (like index and sum) and returns a boolean
ladders.explanation: Recursion with state parameters is a fundamental building block for dynamic programming problems like subset sum. In this context, we define a recursive function that accepts parameters representing the current state of the problem—typically the current index in the array and the current accumulated sum. The function returns a boolean indicating whether a solution exists from that state. The intuition is to break down the problem into smaller, self-similar subproblems: starting from a given state, we reduce the problem size (by advancing the index) and update the state (by possibly adding to the sum). This recursive decomposition continues until we reach a base case that can be solved directly. For subset sum, the state (index, current_sum) uniquely defines a subproblem: "Can we achieve the target sum using elements from index onward, given that we have already accumulated current_sum?" The recursive calls explore transitions from this state: by including the current element, we move to state (index+1, current_sum + arr[index]); by excluding, we move to (index+1, current_sum). The base cases are: if current_sum equals target, return True; if index is beyond array length, return False; if current_sum exceeds target, return False (pruning). This building block teaches how to design recursive functions with multiple parameters that capture problem state, which is essential for memoization. Time complexity without optimization is exponential, but this sets the stage for adding memoization in higher levels. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. Connection to L2: This recursion implements the include/exclude decisions of L2. Connection to L4: The basic operation of function calls is used here. Common mistakes include forgetting to pass state parameters correctly, infinite recursion due to missing base cases, or not handling edge cases like empty arrays. Real code pattern: Define a function with parameters index and current_sum, use if statements for base cases, then return recursive calls.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Function Calls with Boolean Return
ladders.desc: Create a simple function that takes inputs and returns True or False based on a condition
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves writing a function that accepts parameters, performs some checks, and returns a boolean value (True or False). In programming, functions are reusable blocks of code that encapsulate logic. For subset sum, we need functions that return booleans to indicate whether a condition is met (e.g., if a sum equals a target). The intuition is to model decision-making as a function that evaluates to True or False, which is foundational for recursive algorithms. We start by defining a function with a clear signature, such as def check_sum(current_sum, target):. Inside, we use an if statement to compare current_sum with target: if they are equal, return True; otherwise, return False. This simple operation teaches how to structure code that returns booleans, which is essential for building up to more complex recursive functions. Time complexity is O(1) for a single comparison. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in the base cases of the recursive function in L3. Connection to L5: It relies on the concept of boolean logic from L5. Common mistakes include not using return statements correctly, confusing assignment (=) with comparison (==), or not handling edge cases like None inputs. Real code pattern: Use def, parameters, if-else, and return True/False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Boolean Logic and Comparisons
ladders.desc: Understand True/False values and how to compare numbers for equality
ladders.explanation: Boolean logic is the foundation of decision-making in programming. A boolean is a data type that can have one of two values: True or False. In the context of subset sum, we use booleans to represent whether a condition is met, such as if a sum equals a target value. Comparisons are operations that evaluate to booleans. For numbers, the equality comparison (==) checks if two numbers are equal and returns True if they are, False otherwise. For example, 5 == 5 returns True, while 3 == 7 returns False. This concept is crucial because recursive algorithms often rely on boolean results to determine base cases and combine results using logical operators like OR (which returns True if at least one operand is True). The intuition is that by breaking down a problem into smaller yes/no questions, we can build up to complex decisions. In subset sum, we ask: "Does this subset sum to the target?" which translates to a boolean answer. Time and space complexity are not applicable here as this is a basic operation. Connection to L4: This foundation is used in the function that returns booleans in L4. Common mistakes include confusing = (assignment) with == (comparison), not understanding that True and False are keywords (not strings), or misusing boolean operators. Real code pattern: Use expressions like a == b directly in if statements or return statements.

approaches.name: DP with Tabulation O(n * sum)
approaches.summary: Build a 2D boolean table where dp[i][j] indicates if sum j can be achieved using the first i elements. Iteratively fill the table based on inclusion or exclusion of elements.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Subset Sum Existence with DP Tabulation
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if any subset sums to target using 2D DP table.
ladders.explanation: The subset sum existence problem asks: given an array of non-negative integers and a target sum, can we select some elements (possibly none) that sum exactly to the target? This is a classic NP-complete problem, but for moderate target values, dynamic programming provides an efficient solution. The DP with tabulation approach builds a 2D boolean table dp[i][j] where i ranges from 0 to n (number of elements) and j ranges from 0 to target. dp[i][j] = true means we can achieve sum j using some subset of the first i elements. The table is filled iteratively using two key observations: 1) If we can achieve sum j using first i-1 elements, we can also achieve it using first i elements (by excluding the i-th element). 2) If we can achieve sum j-arr[i-1] using first i-1 elements, we can achieve sum j using first i elements (by including the i-th element). The base case dp[0][0] = true (empty subset gives sum 0). The final answer is dp[n][target]. Time complexity is O(n*target) as we fill n+1 rows and target+1 columns. Space complexity is O(n*target) for the table, though can be optimized to O(target) using 1D array. This approach works because it systematically explores all possible subset combinations without explicit enumeration, using optimal substructure property: solution for dp[i][j] depends only on solutions for smaller i and j. Common mistakes include: forgetting base case dp[0][0]=true, incorrect indexing (arr[i-1] vs arr[i]), and integer overflow for large targets. The solution connects to L1 by using the table filling subroutine as its core component.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[7][10] (n=6, target=9). Base: dp[0][0]=true. Fill row i=1 (element 3): For j=0-9: dp[1][0]=true (exclude), dp[1][3]=true (include since dp[0][0]=true). Row i=2 (34): Copy previous row (exclude), for j>=34 check inclusion but j max=9 so no new trues. Row i=3 (4): Copy, dp[3][4]=true (include dp[2][0]), dp[3][7]=true (include dp[2][3]). Row i=4 (12): Copy, j>=12 no new. Row i=5 (5): Copy, dp[5][5]=true (include dp[4][0]), dp[5][8]=true (include dp[4][3]), dp[5][9]=true (include dp[4][4]) → target achieved! Row i=6 (2): Continue but already true. Final dp[6][9]=true.

Initialize dp[4][5]. Base dp[0][0]=true. Row i=1 (1): dp[1][0]=true, dp[1][1]=true. Row i=2 (2): dp[2][0]=true, dp[2][1]=true, dp[2][2]=true (include dp[1][0]), dp[2][3]=true (include dp[1][1]). Row i=3 (7): dp[3][0]=true, dp[3][1]=true, dp[3][2]=true, dp[3][3]=true, for j>=7 check inclusion but dp[2][j-7] negative so no new. dp[3][4] remains false. Final answer false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Filling DP Table Row
ladders.desc: Compute one row of DP table given previous row and current element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the subset sum DP solution. Given: previous row prev (boolean array of size target+1 representing dp[i-1][*]), current element value, target sum. Compute new row curr (dp[i][*]). The algorithm: Initialize curr as copy of prev (exclusion case). Then for each sum j from value to target: if prev[j-value] is true, set curr[j]=true (inclusion case). This works because to achieve sum j using element value, we need to have achieved sum j-value without it. The intuition: we're extending subset possibilities by considering adding the current element to previously achievable sums. Time O(target) per row, space O(target) for two rows. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called n times to build full table. Common mistakes: starting j loop from 0 instead of value (negative index), not copying prev first, or modifying prev in-place. The pseudocode: def fill_row(prev, value, target): curr = prev.copy(); for j in range(value, target+1): if prev[j-value]: curr[j]=True; return curr.

examples.input: prev = [True, False, False, False], value = 2, target = 3
examples.trace: Start curr = [T,F,F,F]. j=2: prev[0]=T → curr[2]=T. j=3: prev[1]=F → curr[3] stays F. Final curr = [T,F,T,F].

examples.input: prev = [True, True, False, False], value = 1, target = 3
examples.trace: curr = [T,T,F,F]. j=1: prev[0]=T → curr[1]=T (already T). j=2: prev[1]=T → curr[2]=T. j=3: prev[2]=F → curr[3]=F? Wait prev[2] is F, but check: prev[2] corresponds to j-value=3-1=2, which is F, so curr[3] remains F. Actually output should be [T,T,T,F]. Let's correct: After j=2, curr=[T,T,T,F]. j=3: prev[2]=F → no change. So final [T,T,T,F].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inclusion/Exclusion Principle for Subsets
ladders.desc: Determine if sum j achievable with first i elements using decisions on i-th element.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental recurrence relation for subset sum DP: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] OR (j >= arr[i-1] AND dp[i-1][j-arr[i-1]]). It states that to achieve sum j using first i elements, we have two options: 1) Exclude the i-th element: then we must have achieved sum j using first i-1 elements (dp[i-1][j]). 2) Include the i-th element: then we must have achieved sum j-arr[i-1] using first i-1 elements, and also j must be at least arr[i-1] to include it. The OR combines these possibilities. Intuition: This breaks the problem into smaller subproblems, using optimal substructure—the solution for i elements depends only on solutions for i-1 elements. This is classic dynamic programming thinking: build solution incrementally. Time/space for just this logic is O(1) per cell, but overall O(n*target). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the row-filling subroutine. Common mistakes: Using AND instead of OR, incorrect array indexing, or missing the j >= arr[i-1] condition. Pseudocode: if j >= arr[i-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] or dp[i-1][j-arr[i-1]] else: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j].

examples.input: dp[i-1] row: [T, F, T, F], arr[i-1]=2, compute dp[i][3]
examples.trace: j=3, arr[i-1]=2. Check inclusion: j>=2 true, dp[i-1][1]=F. Exclusion: dp[i-1][3]=F. OR gives F? Wait recalc: dp[i-1][j-arr[i-1]] = dp[i-1][1]=F, dp[i-1][j]=dp[i-1][3]=F, so OR is F. But output should be F. Let's correct example: Use dp[i-1]=[T,T,F,F], arr[i-1]=1, j=2. Inclusion: j>=1 true, dp[i-1][1]=T. Exclusion: dp[i-1][2]=F. OR gives T.

examples.input: dp[i-1] row: [T, F, F, F], arr[i-1]=3, compute dp[i][3]
examples.trace: j=3, arr[i-1]=3. Inclusion: j>=3 true, dp[i-1][0]=T → inclusion true. Exclusion: dp[i-1][3]=F. OR gives true.

ladders.title: Building Block — Boolean Array Manipulation for Sum Ranges
ladders.desc: Create and update a boolean array representing achievable sums up to target.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves working with a 1D boolean array dp of size target+1, where dp[j] indicates if sum j is achievable using elements processed so far. Initially, only dp[0]=true (empty set sum 0). When processing a new element with value v, we update dp: for j from target down to v, if dp[j-v] is true, set dp[j]=true. The downward iteration prevents overwriting values needed for same element reuse (though subset sum doesn't allow reuse, downward is still good practice). Intuition: We're propagating truth values through the sum space—each element opens new achievable sums by adding v to previously achievable sums. Time O(target) per element, space O(target). Connection to L2: This array implements the inclusion/exclusion logic in 1D. Common mistakes: Iterating upward (causing multiple uses of same element), not handling v > target, or incorrect initialization. Pseudocode: dp = [False]*(target+1); dp[0]=True; for v in arr: for j in range(target, v-1, -1): if dp[j-v]: dp[j]=True.

Time O(1), space O(1) per operation. Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in the boolean array updates. approaches.name: DP Space-Optimized O(n * sum)
approaches.summary: Optimize the tabulation approach by using a 1D array to track achievable sums, updating it in reverse to avoid overwriting. Best for large n when sum is manageable.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Subset Sum Existence with Space-Optimized DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if any subset sums to target using 1D DP array with reverse iteration.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete subset sum existence problem solved using space-optimized dynamic programming. The core insight is that we only need to track which sums are achievable up to the current element, not the entire history. We maintain a boolean array dp where dp[s] = True if sum s can be formed with elements processed so far. The optimization comes from iterating backwards through the dp array when updating for each new element. This prevents overwriting values we need for the current iteration. The intuition: when considering element arr[i], we want to know which sums we could achieve WITHOUT arr[i] (from previous iteration) and which we can achieve WITH arr[i] (by adding arr[i] to previously achievable sums). By iterating backwards from target down to arr[i], we ensure we're using dp values from the previous iteration (before arr[i] was considered) when computing dp[s] = dp[s] OR dp[s-arr[i]]. This gives us O(n * sum) time complexity but only O(sum) space complexity, making it practical for large n when sum is manageable. The algorithm: 1. Initialize dp[0] = True (empty subset gives sum 0), others False. 2. For each element x in arr: For s from target down to x: dp[s] = dp[s] OR dp[s-x]. 3. Return dp[target]. Common mistakes include iterating forwards (causes multiple uses of same element), not handling large sums efficiently, and forgetting to initialize dp[0]. This connects to L1 as the complete application of the reverse iteration technique.

examples.trace:
Initialize dp[0..9]: dp[0]=True, others False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Reverse Iteration DP Update
ladders.desc: The core update step: for each element, update dp array backwards to avoid overwriting.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the critical optimization in the space-optimized subset sum algorithm. Given a current dp array representing achievable sums before considering a new element x, we need to update it to include possibilities with x. The naive approach would use a separate array, but we can do it in-place by iterating from target down to x. The intuition: dp[s] should become True if either it was already True (without x) OR dp[s-x] was True before considering x (meaning we can add x to that sum). If we iterate forwards from x to target, when we compute dp[s] = dp[s] OR dp[s-x], the dp[s-x] might have been updated in the same iteration (if s-x >= x), meaning we might use x multiple times. By iterating backwards, we ensure dp[s-x] comes from the previous state (before x was considered), so each element is used at most once. The algorithm: Input: dp array (size target+1), element x, target. For s from target down to x: dp[s] = dp[s] OR dp[s-x]. Time: O(target) per element, space: O(1) extra. Common mistakes: iterating forwards, not checking if x <= target, or modifying dp while reading it incorrectly. This feeds into L0 as the inner loop of the complete solution.

examples.input: dp = [True, False, False, False], x = 2, target = 3
examples.trace:
Initial dp: index 0=True, 1=False, 2=False, 3=False.
s=3 down to 2:
s=3: dp[3] = dp[3] OR dp[1] = False OR False = False.
s=2: dp[2] = dp[2] OR dp[0] = False OR True = True.
Final dp: [True, False, True, False].

s=2: dp[2] = F OR dp[1]=T = T.
s=1: dp[1] = T OR dp[0]=T = T.
Final dp: [T, T, T, F]. Wait, dp[3] still False? But with x=1 and dp[2] now True, we should get dp[3]=True. Error: Because we iterate backwards, when s=3, dp[2] is still False (from initial state). So dp[3] remains False. This is correct: we only use x once, so from sums {0,1} and x=1, we can get {0,1,2} but not 3. So output [T, T, T, F].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Achievable Sums Tracking with 1D Array
ladders.desc: Maintain a 1D boolean array where dp[s] indicates if sum s is achievable.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental DP state representation: dp[s] = True if sum s can be formed using a subset of the elements processed so far. The intuition is that we build up achievable sums incrementally. Start with only sum 0 achievable (empty subset). For each new element x, the new set of achievable sums is the union of: 1) All sums achievable without x (previous dp), and 2) All sums achievable by adding x to previously achievable sums (i.e., s where dp[s-x] was True). This gives the recurrence: new_dp[s] = old_dp[s] OR old_dp[s-x] if s >= x. The key is we only need the previous state, not all history, allowing space optimization. The algorithm: Initialize dp[0]=True, others False. For each x: create new_dp as copy of dp. For s from x to target: new_dp[s] = new_dp[s] OR dp[s-x]. Then dp = new_dp. Time: O(n * target), space: O(target) for two arrays. Common mistakes: not initializing dp[0], incorrect bounds, or misunderstanding the recurrence. This connects to L1 by showing the state before optimization to reverse iteration.

examples.trace:
Initialize dp: [T, F, F, F, F, F] (size 6 for sums 0..5).

Final dp: [T, F, T, T, F, T].

ladders.title: Building Block — Subset Sum Recurrence Relation
ladders.desc: Define whether sum s is achievable using first i elements: dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s] OR dp[i-1][s-arr[i]].
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the classic DP recurrence for subset sum. Let dp[i][s] be True if sum s can be formed using some subset of the first i elements (arr[0..i-1]). Base case: dp[0][0] = True (empty set gives sum 0), dp[0][s>0] = False. Recurrence: For i>0, dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s] OR dp[i-1][s-arr[i-1]] if s >= arr[i-1], else dp[i-1][s]. Intuition: To achieve sum s with first i elements, we either don't use the i-th element (dp[i-1][s]) or we use it, in which case we need to achieve s-arr[i-1] with the first i-1 elements. This builds a 2D table where rows represent elements and columns represent sums. The algorithm: Initialize dp[0][0]=True. For i from 1 to n: For s from 0 to target: if s < arr[i-1]: dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s]; else: dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s] OR dp[i-1][s-arr[i-1]]. Time: O(n * target), space: O(n * target). Common mistakes: off-by-one indices, missing base case, or incorrect recurrence. This feeds into L2 by providing the foundation for space optimization.

So dp[2][5]=True.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Boolean OR with Array Lookup
ladders.desc: Compute dp[s] = dp[s] OR dp[s-x] for given indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental operation in the DP update: given a boolean array dp and indices s and s-x, set dp[s] to True if either it was already True or dp[s-x] is True. This implements the logical OR operation. Intuition: We're combining two possibilities—either sum s was already achievable, or we can achieve it by adding the current element x to an achievable sum s-x. The operation must be done carefully to avoid using updated values incorrectly. The algorithm: Check if s-x is within bounds (>=0). Then dp[s] = dp[s] OR dp[s-x]. In pseudocode: if s >= x: dp[s] = dp[s] or dp[s-x]. Time: O(1), space: O(1). Common mistakes: accessing out-of-bounds indices, not using boolean logic correctly, or misunderstanding the operation's purpose. This feeds into L3 as the core step in the recurrence relation.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Boolean Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Create and manipulate a boolean array where each index represents a sum.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves working with a boolean array (or list) where each element dp[i] is either True or False. In subset sum, we use such an array to track achievable sums: dp[s] = True if sum s can be formed. Key operations: initialization (set all to False, then dp[0]=True), accessing elements by index, and updating values. Intuition: The array serves as a lookup table—instead of storing all subsets, we compress information into which sums are possible. The algorithm: 1. Create array dp of size target+1. 2. Set all elements to False. 3. Set dp[0] = True. 4. To check if sum s is achievable, read dp[s]. 5. To update, set dp[s] = True. Time for access/update: O(1), space: O(target). Common mistakes: incorrect array size, off-by-one errors, or misunderstanding boolean values. This feeds into L4 as the data structure used in the DP operations.
